title: Who actually spends India's money?
tension: India grows on its own people's shopping, not on factories building or the world buying. Nearly two-thirds of GDP is households at the kirana, the chemist, the phone shop. That is unusual for a poor country trying to grow fast: the East Asian tigers grew by investing furiously and exporting hard, while India leans on the domestic consumer. It makes growth resilient when the world wobbles, but it also means a low investment share and a permanent trade deficit, and it raises the real question: can a country get rich mainly by buying from itself?
teachingGoal: Let a reader take GDP apart from the demand side and see who does the spending: households, businesses (investment), government, and the world (net exports). They should understand why India is consumption-led, what the investment rate signals, why net exports are negative, where the money to invest comes from (saving), and why a consumption-led path is both a cushion and a constraint.
sectionArc: If you add up all the spending, who is doing it? (the demand-side view: consumption + investment + government + net exports; the four shares)
Why is the Indian consumer the engine? (private consumption near two-thirds of GDP; what households buy; consumption-led growth)
How much does India invest in its own future? (gross fixed capital formation around 30%; why economists watch the investment rate as the seed of tomorrow's growth)
What does the government actually spend? (government consumption around a tenth; what 'government consumption' means versus government investment)
Why does India buy more from the world than it sells? (exports vs imports; the negative net-exports wedge; contrast with export-led East Asia)
Where does the money to invest come from? (saving funds investment; India's high saving rate; households, not firms or the state, do most of it, through deposits, gold, provident funds)

So is a consumption-led economy a strength or a weakness? (resilience when global demand falls, but lower investment and a trade gap; the live question of whether consumption alone can carry a poor country to riches)
mustTeach: GDP from the demand side = private consumption + investment + government spending + net exports
private final consumption is the largest component in India, around 61% of GDP
gross fixed capital formation (investment) is around 30% of GDP and is what economists watch for future growth
net exports are negative because imports exceed exports
domestic investment is financed by domestic saving, and Indian households do most of the saving
a consumption-led growth model is unusual and is both a cushion and a constraint
featureNumbers: Private consumption as % of GDP, latest
Private consumption as % of GDP, earliest
Gross fixed capital formation as % of GDP, latest
Government consumption as % of GDP, latest
Net exports as % of GDP, latest
Gross saving rate (% of GDP), latest
Household saving as % of GDP, latest
Private-corporate saving as % of GDP, latest
Public-sector saving as % of GDP, latest

contextCards.fact: The economies that grew fastest in the late 20th century, notably China and South Korea, did it through unusually high investment, often 35 to 45 percent of GDP, and by running trade surpluses, selling more to the world than they bought. India's growth has leaned the other way, on domestic household consumption, with a lower investment share and a persistent trade deficit.
contextCards.source: World Bank national accounts; standard growth-economics literature (widely established)

contextCards.fact: A gross national saving rate around 30 percent of GDP is high by world standards, where many rich economies save 20 percent or less. In India, households rather than companies or the government do the bulk of the saving, and they hold it largely as bank deposits, gold, provident and pension funds, insurance, and property.
contextCards.source: World Bank / national accounts; standard (widely established)

contextCards.fact: There is no forced 100% composition here because net exports are negative; present the expenditure breakdown as a table, not a stacked bar.
